## [IGML/IGML User Guide/Shots MezQL User Guide/Shots User Guide/Logging/Shots Waterfall Logging](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/IGML/IGML_User_Guide/Shots_MezQL_User_Guide/Shots_User_Guide/Logging/Shots_Waterfall_Logging/)
HOW TO ENABLE? Define the scuba subset same and sampling rate in your client config. shots=ShotsServiceConfig( client=ServiceClientConfig(...), logging_config=ShotsLoggingConfig( scuba_subset_name=<Clients_scuba_table_name>, rule_tag_value_sample_rate=<Desired_Sampling_Rate>)), This will create and log to per client scuba table: shots_waterfall_stage_results_<Clients_scuba_table_name> with the specified sampling rate. Example: shots=ShotsServiceConfig( client=ServiceClientConfig(...), logging_config=ShotsLoggingConfig( scuba_subset_name="fast_fallback_feed", rule_tag_value_sample_rate=0.01)), This will log the data of this client to scuba table: shots_waterfall_stage_results_fast_fallback_feed with the given sampling rate of 0.01. Notes: Please ensure your scuba subset name is unique. Perhaps it can be same as your client id or maybe different but then try to prefix with something that will uniquely identify your client / product. Create the table from scuba UI, using scuba table: shots_waterfall_stage_results_<client_scuba_table_name>, and scribe perfpipe_shots_waterfall_stage_results_<client_scuba_table_name> will be automatically created. The client oncall team should own the table and scribe, and request quota as needed HOW TO OVERWRITE SAMPLING RATE? Sometimes we may need a holistic view of a request across multiple services and if a request is chosen to log by a service, it may be beneficial to log this request in other downstream services. To achieve this, a per request knob to force enable/disable logging of the request is provided. HOW TO SPECIFY THE TAG-VALUE TO LOG IN THE MEZQL? For instance, if you are interested in understanding the media-type distribution in sourcing stage, here is how to write the query to let Shots log all entities' media-type in discoverable_media rule. viewer.follows.discoverable_media(output_tags=["recomender_type"]) `tags_to_log=[media_type]` Then the rule call of discoverable_media will generate a row in the waterfall logging table, with media_type tag being a column. Optionally you can also specify trace=[trace_key] in additional to tags_to_log`, which will be populated into the "trace key" column. HOW TO INCREASE THE RETENTION? Virtual Retention: Virtual retention allows users to extend their table retention by using a backing hive table and seamlessly querying both tables and combining data. This will make queries within retention as fast as usual and queries past retention will have an increased latency. Please follow this instruction to enable the virtual retention. Request more capacity from Scuba: Please follow this instruction to increase the scuba retention via requesting capacity. Decrease sampling rate: Please check previous section to see how to update this table's scuba table. HOW TO TEST? If you're changing the core waterfall logging logic or adding a custom use case, you should follow the following steps to test: Correctness: Build Shots locally with your changes and 100% waterfall logging. Send a test query and verify in the waterfall logging scuba that the logged results look accurate. Attach a screenshot of the waterfall results and a link to the Scuba as part of the test plan. Reliability: Trigger a Shots SL with your changes and 100% waterfall logging. This can be done as part of a diff (e.g. D52131233). This will hopefully expose any crashes. If you want to check whether your changes significantly regress latency, CPU, or errors, you should trigger a Shots SL with prod changes and 100% waterfall logging. You can then compare results for the prod waterfall costs versus your new waterfall costs.

## [Monitoring/Monitoring Labs/Lab 2: Logging to SCUBA](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Monitoring/Monitoring_Labs/Lab_2%3A_SCUBA/)
Notice! For production code, see https://www.internalfb.com/intern/wiki/Server_Logging/Loggers_and_Logger_Features/Language_specific_loggers/ Goals In this lab, we will cover: How to create a SCUBA Table. Best practices for setting up SCUBA logging. How to log values to the SCUBA. Part 0: Pre-lab Setup This lab is an extension of the Thrift Lab, and requires familarity with fbcode. If you are unfamiliar with these, you might want to try the following bootcamp labs first: Step 0: Reserving a devserver Step 1: Fbcode lab Step 2: Thrift lab In this lab, we'll be adding SCUBA logging to the ChatRoomService from the Thrift Lab. If you completed the lab yourself, feel free to build on your solution. Otherwise, you can start from the solution in the fbcode/tutorials/thrift/server/impl/ folder. A reference solution is provided at the end of this lab: Reference Solution. Part 1: Creating a SCUBA Table In order to log data to SCUBA, we first need to create a SCUBA table. To do this, navigate to the iData UI and click "Create a New Data Asset" -> Scuba: Next, you will need to provide details for your dataset: Dataset Name - The name of your table. Hive namespace name - The "namespace" that your dataset lives in. This becomes relevant if you later want to persist your Scuba data to Hive. Generally, your Hive namespace should match the namespace of your team's other tables. Actors - The subject that the data is about Data universe - The product or app that the data is from, or whether the use case is for operational purposes Oncall Rotation - The oncall rotation which owns this Scuba table; typically your own team's oncall Attribution Node - The group to attribution this table's usage towards. This will be recommended based on your selected oncall. If you have no recommendation (very unlikely), please select the attribution node closest to your team's oncall using the typeahead.You must be a member of the Oncall Rotation chosen. If you are not a member of any Oncall Rotations, please reach out to your manager to be added to one. For the purposes of the lab, you should use the following values: Dataset Name_bootcamp Hive Namespaceinfrastructure ActorsApplication Data UniverseOPERATIONAL Oncall Rotation(select your oncall here) Attribution Node(select your attribution node here) After the window prompts with success in creating the table, what it does under-the-hood is: It sets up a Scribe category (which you can check via bunnylol command scribe perfpipe__bootcamp) that enables data ingestion into this table. It sets up some basic metadata in iData, which you can check via bunnylol command idata _bootcamp (this could be delayed, but it's fine to move on to the next step) It sets up a cloud object tied to your attribution node so that we know who is responsible for the dataset.This newly created Scuba table doesn't have any data yet, so don't worry if this table hasn't shown up in various internal tools. As long as the Scribe category perfpipe__bootcamp has been successfully created, you can move on to the next section of this lab. Part 2: Creating a SCUBA Logger Class When implementing SCUBA logging within your service, it is generally best practice to create an internal library which handles logging. To create a SCUBA Logger, complete the following steps: In the /tutorials/thrift/server/impl/ folder, create ChatRoomScubaLogger.h and ChatRoomScubaLogger.cpp,fbsource/fbcode/tutorials/thrift/server/impl ├── ChatRoomScubaLogger.cpp ├── ChatRoomScubaLogger.h └── ... then define a corresponding cpp_library Buck target (Buck lab).# tutorials/thrift/server/impl/TARGETS cpp_library( name = "ChatRoomScubaLogger", srcs = [ "ChatRoomScubaLogger.cpp", ], ) Add the following dependencies to your target:"//rfe/scubadata:scubadata" "//monitoring/common:scuba_sample_builder" "//tutorials/thrift/server/impl/if:ChatRoomServerLib-cpp2" ScubaData is a library to construct and log a Scuba Sample (a row in a Scuba table), and ScubaSampleBuilder is a convenient wrapper to add some canonical fields to your Scuba Sample.#

## [Scuba unstructured | Instagram Engineering](https://www.internalfb.com/intern/staticdocs/igwiki/docs/server/topics/logging/scuba-unstructured)
Scuba unstructured | Instagram Engineering | The preferred way of logging to Scuba from Instagram is by using Falco or LoggerConfig. | Topics Logging Scuba unstructured On this page Scuba unstructured caution The preferred way of logging to Scuba from Instagram is by using Falco or LoggerConfig . Unstructured logging is prone to type errors, can't be shared with other repos, and is generally less supported. If your logging contains user data, please use Falco or LoggerConfig. Scuba unstrucutured data will only support operational loggings. Tutorial on logging events with unstructured data from Instagram to Scuba. ​ Falco & Logger provide ways to log fully schematized events to scuba. In the meantime, we often log messages to scuba while the data is unstructured. Hence we created a light-weighted API to enable logging un-schematized data to scuba. This enables developers to add scuba logging in a very handy way. It is recommended to create your own scuba table because it gives you better isolation and in general better control of your data. You should create your own scuba table if you are to log mission-critical data (e.g. data used by detectors, long-term debugging data). Create New Scuba Tables ​ Check out DYIScubaEvent as an example. Follow these steps to set up the pipeline: Register your scribe category, perfpipe_<scuba table name> here . For the following example, let us say the Scuba table name we want to created is ig_user_actions . Note: The perfpipe_ prefixed scribe category is a special type that scuba's data pipeline follows and automatically tails so it shows up in scuba. The table isn't actually created until data is flowing to it from prod. Create a class inherited from ScubaEventBase class. Set TABLE value, in this case ig_user_actions . Fill in NAMES set. They are the list of event names. Fill in FIELDS set. They are field names and types associated with events. Check that your file is filled out correctly. The end result should look something like this (or like any of these files ): from scuba . scuba_event_base import ( ScubaEventBase , ScubaField , ScubaType , ) class UserActionsEvent ( ScubaEventBase ) : TABLE = 'ig_user_actions' NAMES = { 'follow' , } FIELDS = { ScubaField ( name = 'user_id' ) , ScubaField ( name = 'timestamp' , stype = ScubaType . INT ) , } user_actions_event = UserActionsEvent ( ) Now the table needs a JustKnob to enable data writing: Create an integer knob at legacy_scuba with name <scuba table name> , in this case ig_user_actions - make sure to hit "Save" . A JustKnob with the same name as your datatable is needed for data to start getting logged to the table. Set the initial value of the integer knob to 100 to allow 1% traffic to scuba. It defaults to 0, so you need to set the Knob to enable traffic. After creating the JustKnob, add a logging callsite like so: from scuba . user_actions_event import user_actions_event user_actions_event . log ( event_name = 'follow' , user_id = user_id , timestamp = int ( time . time ( ) ) , ) NOTE: If you follow the DYIScubaEvent example above, make sure to use log_event() instead of log(), as the latter will skip any custom logic in your event file Commit and Monitor ​ Commit your code and monitor that: Your scribe category's traffic is below it's limit. The data is available in Scuba (type bunnylol scuba <your_table_name> and set Message Type to the event name). You may need to wait for some time before the data is populated to Scuba. Success rate on ODS: scuba.log_error.<your scuba table>_<your event name> scuba.log_success.<your scuba table> e.g. https://fburl.com/w28pyiiw Gradually decrease the sampling knob value to 1. Keep monitoring the two items as you decrease the sampling. NOTE: If you want to test your logging on your devserver before you commit, create a new scribe category perfpipe_<your scuba table>_sandbox and after a few logs from your devserver you'll be able to see that scuba table with the sandbox postfix. This keeps dev logs from ending up in prod. Event-Level Sampling ​ By default no events are actually logged to

# Do I need a LoggerConfig for a Scuba only table?

## [Signal Loss Feature Mitigation/Engineering Misc/Step-by-step guide to add columns to scuba table](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Signal_Loss_Feature_Mitigation/Engineering_Misc/Step-by-step_guide_to_add_columns_to_scuba_table/)
In this guide, we show step-by-step process to add columns to a scuba table, including all testing steps. We follow closely to the example diff D26414908, where we added signal loss related traces to AdIndexer Waterfall Scuba. Before we continue, there are a few things you should have at hand: Prerequisite know where and how the new trace is calculated, and how to get the reference; know where your destination table is defined. One way to find this information is to code search in fbcode on one of the columns in your target table. Use TRACE_<underscore_delimited_column_name> as the search keyword. If still cannot find your table, please contact the corresponding Infra team POC to get this information. Step 1: Define your trace Find where the traces are defined for the target table, in the example diff, we want to add column to AdIndexer Waterfall, the traces are defined in /data/users/unixname/fbsource/fbcode/admarket/adindexer/utrace/WaterfallUtrace.h; and /data/users/unixname/fbsource/fbcode/admarket/adindexer/utrace/WaterfallUtrace.cpp If you don’t know where to find those files, contact infra POC. In trace head file Depending on the types of the new trace, you may want to use DEFINE_UTRACE_BOOL(trace_name) DEFINE_UTRACE_INT(trace_name, {}) DEFINE_UTRACE_NORMAL_STRING(trace_name) In trace cpp file Here just add &TRACE_trace_name. Step 2: Add your column to scuba Find the file where columns are calculated, in this example, fbcode/admarket/adindexer/AdIndexerWaterfall.cpp. First, take a quick look through the functions that begin with "trace", if the new columns fit into one of the functions, add them there; otherwise, write a new function, add your new columns and make sure you call it after definition. After resolving all linter error, commit and submit your diff. Step 3: Test your new columns The canary test seems to be deprecated. We now switch to ServiceLab for testing. wiki: https://www.internalfb.com/intern/wiki/Ads_Feature_Serving/Testing_changes/ https://www.internalfb.com/intern/wiki/AdsDevEfficiency/ServiceLabForAds/User_Guides/Create_Experiment/ The service lab can be initiated through UI and command line. Method 1: UI Go to the diff page and look for the utility button on top right corner: In the next page, select the correct workload (the default is admarket_adfinder), in this example, we select admarket_adindexer: Double check your experiment settings are filled correctly. Typically, experiment a corresponds to master branch, and experiment b correspond to your diff/revision. Then start your experiment and wait for its completion. Method 2: command line This way is much more straightforward: cd ~/fbsource/fbcode servicelab create admarket_adindexer --local-commit Similarly, make sure you select the correct workload. Once the service lab experiments complete, check if all tests passed. And check the canary scuba table by using the links at the bottom of the service lab page: Check and make sure the desired columns are in the scuba table. FAQ I cannot add (and other) columns to “Group By” section. Why they are not group-able/ aggregat-able? How do I modify their properties? First check your columns in scuba page. If your columns are not group-able, similar to the column “Signal Loss Impacted” below, they cannot be added to “Group By” section. A manual change is required to make them group-able: Go to the idata page of the target table (bunnylol idata <table_name>). Scroll to “Table Content” Section. Find your target columns and click “action”. Modify properties as desired.

## [FBDetect/Onboarding](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/FBDetect/Onboarding/)
The following flow is what a fully onboarded customer can expect from FBDetect: To put it into words: Your regressions will be detected, Best-effort triaged to a root cause, A regression task will be filed, You can manage regressions using a UI Getting to this end-state though is a journey, not a flip of a switch. Getting Started Describe Your Use Case In order for FBDetect to better understand your use case and therefore provide better help, please post a brief description of your use case in FBDetect group when reaching out. For example, you can include information about: What anomalies you are trying to detect. If you can provide some examples, that's even better. Basic information about the data used for detection: where are they stored, what is the volume, ... Start With Detection Our guidance to new customers onboarding onto FBDetect is to focus on the Detect step first. All FBDetect customers get the Alert and Manage steps pretty much out of the box. This means, if you get the Detect step right, you can productionize your setup and start receiving FBDetect alerts right away. Triage step tends to be a bit more involved. It involves bringing in additional context about your use case to help our automated triaging mechanism, AutoRCA, root cause the regression. This "additional context" is typically in the form of datasets that contain contextual data. Examples of such datasets are "a diff-to-modified-functions" dataset and profiling datasets like Xenon, Indigo Tao, PyPerf and Strobelight . AutoTriage already supports common use cases like WWW/fbcode/IG-distillery diffs and config changes like GK/Sitevars. If your triaging needs are met by what we offer, you will be able to easily enable the Triage step. If not, we'll provide you necessary code pointers & guidance to build in support for your use case. Dedicate at least one eng You will want to dedicate at least engineer to drive the onboarding for your use case. Depending on the complexity of your use case, you may want to dedicate more than one engineer. Modes of collaboration Diffs - FBDetect engs are subscribed to all diffs that touch our codepaths. We will provide feedback on diffs. WorkChat - Besides diffs, our primary mode of communication is via WorkChat. We have one main "FBDetect" chat for ongoing project-related communication. It includes FBDetect engs and customer team engs who are using FBDetect. During onboarding, we also typically set up customer-specific chat threads. Workplace - We also have a Workplace group for discussion and announcements. Onboarding Steps FBDetect onboarding is done largely via Configerator configs. FBDetect support different data sources (ODS, Scuba, and Presto) and each has a slightly different onboarding process than others. Steps 1-5 describe the general flow, with links to data source specific information. If what you want is not provided out of the box, please talk to FBDetect team and see what additional code changes are needed. Step 1. Prepare Data This step is to ensure data are properly populated and ready for FBDetect consumption. This can be optional depending on the data source: For ODS, please read this section. For Scuba, nothing needs to be done. For Presto, please read this section. Step 2. Create a FBDetect Config FBDetect workflows are driven by configs. The following steps aim at creating a simple config, so optional fields with default values are not included. If you want to customize them, please refer to the definition of field and pick the values accordingly. 2-1. Create a new config file All configs are in Configerator and each system has its own folder. To add a new config, first create a folder for your system (let's call it my_system), and then add a file called fbdetect_system_config.cconf in this new folder. Your file path should look like source/fbdetect/system_configs/my_system/fbdetect_system_config.cconf. 2-2. Copy config templates Next, you can copy this template into your config file and fill in the TODO's following instructions below. import_thrift("fbdetect/config/system_config.thrift", "*")

Add the new logger.set_<new field>() call for the new field added, see example diff D64694203. Test the change on staging table, see test plan in D64694203. Follows a similar flow as above. 1. Add your new column to sysinspector_asic_data_logger_config.thrift and generate the new table. Taking D66212460 as an example, add your new column(s) to the bottom of the sysinspector_asic_data_logger_config.thrift file. Add relevant descriptions. To generate the new table, run: loggercli codegen SysinspectorAsicDataLoggerConfig Package the generated files into a diff and submit. 2. Add your new fields to Rgpu and the SysInspector logger. In a new diff, stacked on-top of the previous one, make your modifications to Rgpu: update the thrift structure in meta.thrift with your new fields. update device_mon3.rs to read the bootd counters through dbus. These files are organized into different structures which hold different pieces of data. For example, CardAlert is for fields which indicate some error occurred on the ASIC, whereas Ecc is for collection of ECC statistics. Try to add your new columns to a struct that makes sense. Now, modify SysInspector log those new fields to your newly-updated logger. in asic.rs, modify the following functions to log your new columns: log_vender_asic_detail(): convert_details_to_scuba_sample() Take D65914024 as example for these actions. ## [Creating New Scuba Columns](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Scuba/user_guide/Querying_to_Scuba/Creating_New_Scuba_Columns/)
NOTE: This topic has duplicate content with Derived Columns in Scuba. The page provides help in creating new query-, mapped, and enum derived columns in iData. Creating New Derived Columns You may want to add a new column to your Scuba table, based on other columns already logged or based on mapped values. You can do this directly through iData (without needing to write a new presenter), creating persistent derived columns that are accessible to everyone viewing your Scuba table. Notice! Because these new columns are generated in the iData UI, they will not be stored on the backend and will not take any extra storage space. However, they may negatively impact the overall query performance on both the backend and frontend. Creating Query-Derived Columns First, let's visit the iData page for defined columns: Bunnylol iData. In the recently viewed panel, click on the name of your table. In the left hand navigation, click on Table Content. To create a new query-derived column: In the left pane, click Table Content. Click the Create New Column button. Under Derived Column, select the column type (i.e. Numeric, Aggregated, String) and enter a name for the column. Enter the SQL query for the derived column. When you are finished, click Confirm. After confirming, the table content is reloaded and the new column can be seen. A new X icon will be visible to allow removal of the derived column in case you do not need it anymore. Considerations When adding a derived column, always start in Samples View in the Scuba UI to ensure your derived columns are exactly what you want. It is more difficult to debug in Table View. If you are expecting the derived column to contain a string, ensure that you change the column type from Numeric to String. If you want your column to be an integer, remember choose Numeric and add CAST_AS_BIGINT to the definition in case the results of the expression are a string. Aggregated derived columns will only work in views that aggregate the values (Table, Time, etc). This column will always have a value '0' in Samples View. Creating Mapped Columns To create a new mapped column: In Scuba, click the table drop-down and select Table settings. This will take you to your table's iData page. In the left pane, click Table Content. Click the Create New Column button. Select Mapped Column. In the Column Name text box, enter the name for the new column. In the Source Column text box, enter the name for the source column. Select the mapping function from the Mapping Function dropdown list. When you are finished, click Confirm. After confirming, the table content is reloaded and the new column can be seen. A new X icon will be visible to allow removal of the derived column in case you do not need it anymore. Creating Enum Columns In Scuba, click the table drop-down and select Table settings. This will take you to your table's iData page. In the left pane, click Table Content. Click the Create New Column button. Select Enum Column. In the Column Name text box, enter the name for the new column. In the Source Column text box, enter the name for the source column. In the Mapping Function text box, enter the name of the mapping function (no parenthesis). When you are finished, click Confirm. Warning! You must give a unique name to your new column, otherwise the creation will fail. Also, make sure both your source column and the mapping function exist, and the function is valid for the source column you chose before confirming. No validation is performed before creating the column and, as with other column creations, you will not be able to edit this later. After confirming, the table content is reloaded and the new column can be seen. A new X icon will be visible to allow removal of the derived column in case you do not need it anymore. See Also Derived Columns PHP Presenters

## [Server Logging/Logger Config Lifecycle/Adding/Updating logger fields](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Server_Logging/Logger_Config_Lifecycle/Adding_updating_logger_fields/)
What are Logger Fields? Fields in the logger config map to the columns in these downstream destinations. A logger can be logging to one or more destinations (hive / scuba). You may want to have certain columns in your destination tables that need to be defined in the logger config. For example, A hive table example_hive and scuba table example_scuba with columns: string foo int bar vec<string> baz will have the fields in the logger config: WWW Loggers final class ExampleLoggerConfig extends LoggerConfig { <<__Override>> public function configure(LoggerFieldDefiner $fd): LoggerFieldDefiner { $this ->setNamespace('example_namespace') ->setHiveTableName('example_hive') ->logAllToHive() ->setScubaDatasetName('example_scuba') ->logAllToScuba(); $fd->string('foo', 'example string for dex example'); $fd->int('bar', 'example int for dex example'); $fd->vector( 'baz', 'example vector of string for dex example', LoggerType::nonNullableString(), ); return $fd; } } Thrift Loggers @ownership.Ownership{oncall_shortname = "dsi_logger"} @logger_config.Description{ description = "Example thrift logger with foo, bar, and baz fields", } @destination.HiveInfo{ table_name = "example_logger", table_namespace = "dsi_logger", } @destination.ScubaInfo{ dataset_name = "example_logger", dataset_namespace = "dsi_logger", } @logger_config.LoggerInfo{ hive = logger_config.LoggerDestinationInfo{sample_rate = 1.0}, scuba = logger_config.LoggerDestinationInfo{sample_rate = 1.0}, lang = [logger_config.Language.CPP, logger_config.Language.PY3], } @data_origin_canonical.Canonical{ value = data_origin_canonical.CanonicalEnum.Onsite, } @universe.Universe{id = universe.UniverseIdentifier.FACEBOOK} @actor_canonicals.Canonicals{values = [actor_canonical.CanonicalEnum.Employee]} struct ExampleLoggerConfig { @logger_config.Description{description = "A string field"} 1: string foo; @logger_config.Description{description = "An integer field"} 2: i64 bar; @logger_config.Description{description = "A list of strings"} 3: list<string> baz; } A top level field in the LoggerConfig is configured through the field definer as a LoggerField while an inner field is configured as a LoggerType. Configure top level fields using the LoggerFieldDefiner while inner fields using LoggerType . In the above example the top level fields are string, int and vec fields while the inner field is nonNullableString logger type. To find out about all possible fields and types that logger supports, refer here. How do I add a new field? Adding a new field is simple, you grab the field definer in your logger config and set the new field. Since these are local changes in your logger config, you need to actualize it to update the downstreams and generated code. You should then follow up by landing this chang e. Renaming a field on the logger config is equivalent to removing that field and adding a new one. Steps: Add a new field in your logger config using the field definer: $fd->string('new_field', 'some new field description'); [Optional] Do Testing by Staging Logger - be aware that if you are logging a new field to scuba, there needs to be atleast 100+ rows containing that d ata for the changes to be reflected. This applies even for testing staging logger in scuba. Run codegen loggercli wiki // A single LoggerConfig $ loggercli codegen < YourLoggerConfig > // A multiplex Logger group $ loggercli codege n --group <YourLoggerGroup> Actualize your logger config if your Logger is not automatically actualized How To Actualize Sync to FBCode if needed Update the callsites with the generated functional / typed logger changes 'new_field' => 'hello world' or $logger->setNewField('hello world'); Amend your changes and land the updated generated code changes. How do I update an existing field? Logger does not allow changing either the name or type of the fields. The description of fields can be updated though. Fields once actualized are set in stone and cannot be mutated. This is primarily due to the underlying limitations of the downstreams (hive tables / ingestion) more than logger infra. However, there

## [Webacademy-log-to-ods-scuba-and-hive-using-logger/LoggerConfig Bootcamp Lab Exercises](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Webacademy-log-to-ods-scuba-and-hive-using-logger/logging-bootcamp-lab/)
is the image title. Log in C++ Log in Python Log in Java How to add a new destination to an existing LoggerConfig How to modify existing fields of a LoggerConfig How to test logging data to scuba. Here are the solutions LoggerConfig provides to address the above requests one by one: you want to land the data faster. You can add another Scuba destination, beyond Hive, by calling →setScubaDatasetName(scuba_table_name)→ logAllToScuba(). However, notice how different destinations have different constraints on field types. For example Scuba only supports 6 field types, while Hive can support 8 types. The table below lists them and shows the corresponding type names in Scuba and Hive. Hack Type Scuba Type Hive Type int int bigint float int double string normal string bool int bool set tagset array vector normvector array map (not supported) map struct (not supported) struct you want to add an additional field LoggerConfig has native support for adding a new field. Let’s add a new field named “succeeded” of type bool in your LoggerConfig.php. However, note that since Scuba does not support boolean type, you need to convert the type to int if you also want to log this field in Scuba.  you want to change the field name Renaming a field is possible but with a little caveat: low level infrastructure (particularly Hive) does not support mutating an already created field. Renaming the field is equivalent to just adding a field with a new name. The field with the old name will continue to exist in Hive but LoggerConfig will stop logging values into those columns. Note: Scuba tables are materialized lazily. Therefore whether the old field shows in Scuba depends on whether you have logged any values under the old name before.  you want to change the type of field Changing type of created field is not supported in LoggerConfig due to the same constraint as above. Therefore the simplest way to address the request is to just add a new field titled “image_id_titles”. However notice that Scuba does not support map typed field, so this column can only be logged in Hive. Now your new LoggerConfig schema looks like the following <<__Override>>   public function configure(LoggerFieldDefiner $fd): LoggerFieldDefiner {       $this       ->setDescription('A LoggerConfig for Bootcamp lab')       ->setNamespace('logger_test')       ->setHiveTableName('bootcamp_lab_image_server_<unixname>')       ->logAllToHive()       ->annotations(         DataOriginCanonical::onsite(), // logging from facebook online product         ActorCanonical::unknown(), // system logs do not have owner       )       ->setScubaDatasetName('bootcamp_lab_image_server_<unixname>')       ->logAllToScuba();            $fd->int('request_id', 'The id of the image search request')       ->annotations(SemanticTypeTechnical::guid());     $fd->string('search_input_keyword', 'The input keyword of the image search request')       ->annotations(SemanticTypeCanonical::ugc_searchQuery());          $fd->bool('succeeded', 'Whether the request succeeded')       ->annotations(SemanticTypeCanonical::misc())       ->toScubaInt();     $fd->map(       'image_id_titles', // name       'The id and title map of images returned', // description       LoggerType::nonNullableInt(),       LoggerType::nonNullableString(),     )       ->annotations(SemanticTypeCanonical::ugc_searchQuery())       ->notToScuba();     return $fd;   } } Now, let's run staging on your modified logger. loggercli stage actualize-stage YourConfigName<without extension .php> # i.e. BootcampLabImageServerLoggerConfig You should see a new scuba destination be added to the staging information printed: ... Staging Information with Logger Config: BootcampLabImageServerLoggerConfig         Staging Hive Table Name: logger_staging_bharatv_27f70ad61eb3c7d87ff57c2c938c775b                 Hive Table(_inc_archive): https://fburl.com/hive/kggukhrz                 Scribe

is sufficient. Source: https://fb.workplace.com/groups/allthelogs/permalink/31064770453144840/ How do I log to Scuba from a Rust binary on macOS, Windows, or corp machines when the generated Rust Logger fails to build? The generated Rust Logger depends on a C++ transport chain that is Linux-only, so it won't build on macOS. Bypassing it with raw Scribe clients (e.g., ScubaSampleBuilder, ScribeCatPoolClient, scribe-thrift-bareclient, ScribeCatClient, scuba_cached, tracing-scuba) will succeed at the Scribe layer but deliver 0 events to Scuba because Data Guard silently drops non-Logger writes. Solution: Check the Logger Elsewhere wiki for how to set up logging from non-server environments. For Rust-specific details, see the Rust Logger wiki. The Rust Logger is owned by MFT Dev Infra — reach out to them for additional platform support requests. Key insight: Data Guard enforces that only Logger-pipeline writes reach Scuba. Any alternative Scribe client will appear to work (writes succeed) but events won't appear in the Scuba table. Source: https://fb.workplace.com/groups/allthelogs/permalink/31113637728258112/ How do I log to Scuba/Hive from Python (non-www)? (writing to hive/scuba works from www but not python, python logger, logging from python script, Py3LoggerConfig) Python logging to Scuba/Hive requires a Py3LoggerConfig (or XplatLoggerConfig implementing Py3LoggerConfig). A Hack-only LoggerConfig defined in www will NOT work from Python. Setup steps: Create or convert to XplatLoggerConfig: Your LoggerConfig PHP class must implement the Py3LoggerConfig interface (in addition to HackLoggerConfig if you also log from www) Run codegen: loggercli codegen YourLoggerConfig — this generates the Python logger code in fbcode Sync to fbcode: Run loggercli sync config YourLoggerConfig or land the auto-sync diff Import in Python: Import the generated logger from the fbcode path and use it in your Python code Common issues: "Works from www but not Python": Your LoggerConfig likely only implements HackLoggerConfig. Add Py3LoggerConfig interface. Generated files missing in fbcode: Run codegen from a megarepo OD (WWW+FBSource+Configerator), not a www-only checkout. Data appears in Scuba from www but not from Python: Ensure you're using the generated Python logger, not a raw Scribe client. Data Guard drops non-Logger writes. Key concept: Each language has its own Logger interface (HackLoggerConfig, CppLoggerConfig, Py3LoggerConfig). Adding a new language requires implementing the corresponding interface and running codegen to generate the language-specific logger code. Use the 'whence' option in genLog: await FooBarFunctionalLogger::genLog( shape('arg1' => 'value1', 'vc' => $vc), shape('whence' => WhenceScribeLogged::PROD) ); This bypasses the automatic sandbox/dev detection and writes directly to production. Source: https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Server_Logging/Loggers_and_Logger_Features/Language_specific_loggers/Hack_Loggers/ How do I use a FunctionalLogger in hphpd or script environment? ## [Server Logging/Logger Config Lifecycle/Adding updating logger fields/Adding struct fields to Scuba](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Server_Logging/Logger_Config_Lifecycle/Adding_updating_logger_fields/Adding_struct_fields_to_Scuba/)
IMPORTANT: The content on this page is only applicable to Hack loggers. LoggerStructScubaHelper class does NOT work with xplat (C++) loggers, as the class implementation uses derivers to transform the struct data. NOTE: Oncall Support : If you have feedback/questions or need help, please reach out to Valery Yundin, Alex Mulchandani, or ig_home_eco_de oncall.. When to use the feature Struct field type is supported in hive, but it's not supported in scuba. Use the feature when your logger config already logs a struct field to hive, and you want to log the data in the struct to scuba. The LoggerStructScubaHelper will help you log the subfields in the struct to scuba as multiple independent fields, a.k.a flattening the struct. The feature is a solution to log Nucleator fields to Scuba. New Implementation - LoggerStructScubaHelper Script Since the process of extracting struct fields and logging to Scuba is streamlined, we've created a script to standardize this process for consumers. To use the script, you just need to already have a LoggerConfig defined/actualized along with the struct/Nucleated fields. See InstagramNotificationEventsGroupSchemaTrait.php and InstagramNotificationEventsScubaStructHelper.php for example e2e implementation. What does the script do phps LoggerStructScubaHelper -c <LOGGER_CONFIG> For XYZLoggerConfig, this script will auto-generate a class with the naming convention XYZScubaStructHelper (e.g. InstagramNotificationEventsScubaStructHelper.php). Make sure to re-run this script anytime you update your Logger's schema. The script generates the following: Arg deriver helper methods Field definer helper methods Wrapper method to ease usage within your LoggerConfig Now the script has been integrated into the loggercli: loggercli codegen-and-generate-scuba-helper <LoggerConfigName> This is equivalent to running loggercli codegen first and then phps LoggerStructScubaHelper. Each struct will have its own "make" methods within the ScubaStructHelper class that you can call within your Logger Config. The make method takes in 4 params: $fd Struct column name A map where the keys are the sub-struct field paths and the values are the names for scuba (or null if you want to use the same name) A prefix you can assign to every scuba column name InstagramNotificationEventsScubaStructHelper::makeNotificationForScuba( $fd, 'notification', Map { 'notification_delivery_id' => null, 'type' => null, 'recipient.fbid' => 'recipient_fbid', }, 'new_prefix', ) Legacy Implementation Scuba does not have native support for struct fields, which makes data propagation a little more difficult than simply using ->toScuba(). The best way to do this is via arg derives. Your LoggerConfig may look something like: final class ExampleLoggerConfig extends LoggerConfig { <<__Override>> public function configure(LoggerFieldDefiner $fd): LoggerFieldDefiner { $this ->setNamespace('example_namespace') ->setHiveTableName('example_hive') ->logAllToHive() ->setScubaDatasetName('example_scuba') ->logAllToScuba(); ContentNucleatedField::configure( $fd, 'content', 'Attributes about the content', ); $fd->string( 'content_type', "The type of content", ) ->setOneArgDeriver(static::genContentType<>, 'content') ->notToHive() ->toScuba() ->annotations(HiveAnonPolicy::not_uii()); return $fd; } public static async function genContentType(

## [RL Lab Infrastructure/Team Internal/Logging to Scuba/Hive (Lab context)](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/RL_Lab_Infrastructure/Team_Internal/Logger_%28Scuba_Hive%29/)
Overview The best way to send data to Scuba and Hive is using Logger. In short, A LoggerConfig must be created for every table. These configs define all field names, types, and destinations. Logger has client libraries that send data to Scribe, where consumers for Scuba and/or Hive read it into their respective tables. Validation is provided client-side and in Scribe. See Server Logging Overview for details. The Device Farm is a lab, so unfortunately we are not able to use the generated Logger libraries (which only work on prod). There are two alternatives: logger_cat and Structured API. logger_cat is similar to scribe_cat, it provides a way to send data without code generation or dependencies. Structured API allows you to log data programmatically, which we prefer. In both cases, the data is sent locally to ScribbleD (the non-prod Scribe daemon installed on every host). It looks like this: StructuredAPILogger Since we can't use the generated libraries, we have to write some code of our own to use Structured API. This has been done generically with StructuredAPILogger. This will work on Linux/Windows/Mac and works for any entry (more on this below). The Device Farm code is using many methods to send data to Scuba. The switch to Logger gives us a good opportunity to unify to a single way. logger_cat If you need to log from a bash script, you can use logger_cat. TODO add example of this once it has been done the first time. Setting up a new table The documentation at Getting Started with Logger is very comprehensive, but there are some nuances for the Device Farm since it is off-prod. 1. Create the LoggerConfig Create from an existing Scribe category: # Deduce logger configuration from existing Scuba table loggercli deduce scuba perfpipe_your_existing_table Or create a new logger config # Create a new logger configuration loggercli new MyDataLoggerConfig bastille 2. Generate Thrift code # Generate thrift code for your logger configuration loggercli codegen MyDataLoggerConfig --generate-log-entry -ll RUST (or PYTHON...) 3. Set up category access to allow non-prod access to Scribe # Add categories to GraphAPI dmv graphapi add-categories -v SCUBA_JSON perfpipe_my_data 4. Add your logger to the the LOGGER_GRAPHAPI_WRITE_ALLOWLIST 5. Add structured_api_logger and the Python/Rust Thrift types synced in step 4 to your BUCK target deps = [ ... "//bastille/utils:structured_api_logger", "//dsi/logger/log_entry/MyDataLoggerConfig:my_data_log_entry-python-types", ... ] 6. Implement in your code using StructuredAPILogger from bastille.utils.structured_api_logger import StructuredAPILogger from meta.logger_config.my_data_logger_config.MyDataLogEntry.thrift_types import MyDataLogEntry # Initialize the logger sapi_logger = StructuredAPILogger( logger_config_name="MyDataLoggerConfig", host="::1", ) # Create an entry entry = MyDataLogEntry( field1="value1", field2="value2", # Add all required fields ) # Log the entry await sapi_logger.log(entry) Example Logger config: D72082074 Sync: D73058480 Allowlist: D73218408 Implement: D73285381 Debugging Once your LoggerConfig has landed, you can use the following bunnylol shortcuts to see what is going on with your data. b logger MyDataLoggerConfig b scribe perfpipe_my_data What about ODS? The Logger overview touches on how it supports ODS. Routing to ODS occurs in the "Server Logging Runtime Stack", which are the generated libraries we don't get to use. Instead, we are using structured API and our own code to skip to Scribe. Unfortunately, this means that Logger support for ODS doesn't help us. Adding fields to existing Logger tables Edit the logger config. This is a .thrift file found at fbcode/dsi/logger/thrift_configs. E.g. fbcode/dsi/logger/thrift_configs/BastilleDeviceHealthchecksLoggerConfig/bastille_device_healthchecks_logger_config.thrift Generate the Logger files: # Generate thrift code for your logger configuration loggercli codegen MyDataLoggerConfig --generate-log-entry -ll RUST (or PYTHON...)

## [Metaconda/Logging and Debugging/Logger](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Metaconda/Logging_and_Debugging/Logger/)
Logger (aka Server Logging) is the standard way of logging data from backend applications to Scuba and Hive at Meta. The high-level workflow is Author a Thrift file in fbcode or Xplat Logger in Hack describing the schema of your logger (what columns are in your table) Run a command to generate some files from that thrift file (including a Python file you will commit in your conda codebase), and land these files to materialize the changes to your Scuba/Hive tables Update your conda code and launch config to log your data You can look at a minimal code example here: https://www.internalfb.com/code/fbsource/genai/msl_infra/conda/examples/logger/ Author your LoggerConfig Have a checkout of fbsource on your devserver, and make sure loggercli is installed (feature install dsi_logger_cli --global). You may author your logger config in fbcode or www repo For fbcode, follow the steps in this wiki to write a Thrift file describing the schema of your LoggerConfig under fbcode/dsi/logger/thrift_configs. You may also author your logger config as xplat logger in WWW repo Codegen and land your Logger code Once your config is ready, run the following command to generate Logger artifacts including Python bindings for your conda codebase: cd ~/fbsource/fbcode # loggercli commands must be run from fbcode loggercli codegen $NameOfYourLoggerConfig --generate-python-conda --conda-path $path_to_your_repo_root for example if you're developing in fbsource/genai/llama4x and logging to MyExampleLoggerConfig (the name of a LoggerConfig is camelCase and always ends in -LoggerConfig), your command will be: loggercli codegen MyExampleLoggerConfig --generate-python-conda --conda-path ~/fbsource/genai/llama4x This will generate a few files inside your Conda codebase (and in fbcode). You need to commit and land those files before you're able to log to your table (because the Scuba/Hive tables will be updated only after that diff lands). In order to create the BUCK target for your generated logger files, use the following command: autodeps2 --caller lint_fixit --ignore-lint-config --remove-deps --generate-all-files --use-all-resolvers $path_to_your_repo_root/logger_configs/generated/$logger_config_name Performance Optimized Logging Python 3.12 has a subprocess performance regression where subprocess.Popen calls are slow in large processes (e.g., with PyTorch models loaded) because fork() must copy page tables. To address this, you can use the --persistent-subprocess flag during codegen, which spawns logger_cat once and keeps it alive across multiple log() calls (~10x faster). loggercli codegen $NameOfYourLoggerConfig --generate-python-conda --conda-path $path_to_your_repo_root --persistent-subprocess Note: This is not the default approach yet while we finalize the implementation. See D90813753 and D90879058 for details. Add your logging code and launch config In your Conda codebase, the most important generated file from the previous step lives at logger_configs/generated/MyExampleLoggerConfig You can use them like this to log data: Synchronous API For synchronous logging, use MyExampleLogger.log(...) from logger_configs.generated.MyExampleLoggerConfig import ( MyExampleLogEntry, MyExampleLogger, ) # ... entry = MyExampleLogEntry(...) MyExampleLogger.log([entry]) Asynchronous API For asynchronous logging, use AsyncMyExampleLogger.log(...) within asyncio event loops and remember to await the log call. Ideal to prevent blocking when logging subprocesses are running. from logger_configs.generated.MyExampleLoggerConfig import ( MyExampleLogEntry, AsyncMyExampleLogger, ) # ... entry = MyExampleLogEntry(...) await AsyncMyExampleLogger.log([entry]) MyExampleLogEntry is a strongly-typed dataclass matching the schema of your LoggerConfig. Note the naming convention: MyExampleLoggerConfig: the name of the config describing your logging schema and targets MyExampleLogEntry: code-generated Python class to hold data to store in individual rows MyExampleLogger: code-generated Python class to log the data AsyncMyExampleLogger: code-generated Python class to log the data

## [Managing logger tables in Scuba](https://www.internalfb.com/wiki/Identity-Matching/Logging_for_Identity/Managing_logger_tables_in_Scuba/)
Scuba is a powerful data analysis tool that allows you to quickly query and visualize large datasets. However, properly managing tables in Scuba is essential, especially as your dataset grows. Creating Scuba Table How to Create Tables in Scuba through Logger Config To create a table in Scuba through Logger Config, follow these steps: In your Logger Config file, specify the scubaDatasetName field with the name of the table you want to create. Define the fields you want to log to Scuba as part of your Logger Config. These fields will become columns in the Scuba table. Run the actualize script to generate the necessary code and update the Scuba table schema. Start logging data to Scuba using your Logger Config. Example: IdentityMatchingServiceRequestLoggerConfig.php $this ->setDescription('Sii only Identity Unified Matching System request log.') ->setNamespace('ad_metrics') ->setHiveTableName('identity_matching_service_request_log') ->setHiveRetention(shape( 'primary_retention' => 30, 'retention_reason' => "This table will be used for UMS request debugging and monitoring.", )) ->logAllToHive() ->setScubaDatasetName('identity_matching_service_request_log') ->logAllToScuba() ->logErrorsToProduction(false); Sampling in Scuba Sampling in scuba can be managed through LoggerConfig files of the respective loggers. For Identity loggers, we have implemented immediate sampling. Immediate Sampling for Identity Loggers Hack Logger: Sampling is managed by implementing method genImmediateSampleRatesTyped. IdentityMatchRequestLoggerConfig.php Logger fields to store the sample rate: $fd->float( 'scuba_sample_rate', 'Scuba downsample rate for genImmediateSampleRatesTyped', ) ->notToHive() ->notToScuba(); $fd->float( 'hive_sample_rate', 'Hive downsample rate for genImmediateSampleRatesTyped', ) ->notToHive() ->notToScuba(); Method to enforce the sampling: public async function genImmediateSampleRatesTyped( shape('scuba_sample_rate' => ?float, 'hive_sample_rate' => ?float) $_input, ): Awaitable<shape( LoggerDestinations::HIVE => float, LoggerDestinations::SCUBA => float, )> { return shape( LoggerDestinations::HIVE => $_input['hive_sample_rate'] ?? 0.0, LoggerDestinations::SCUBA => $_input['scuba_sample_rate'] ?? 0.0, ); } CPP Logger: Sampling is directly managed through FIXED_SAMPLING_RATES via TXplatLoggerConfig. IdentityMatchingServiceRequestLoggerConfig.php const self::TSampleRates FIXED_SAMPLING_RATES = shape(LoggerDestinations::HIVE => 1.0, LoggerDestinations::SCUBA => 0.05); Weight - HITS Weight: The weight column in Scuba represents the number of times a particular event or sample is represented in the dataset. When logging data to Scuba, you can specify a weight for each sample, which indicates how many times that sample should be counted in the overall dataset. For example, if you have a sample with a weight of 10, it means that sample represents 10 identical events or samples in the real world. Hits: The hits column in Scuba represents the number of times a particular event or sample was logged to Scuba. In other words, it's the count of how many times Scuba received a log entry for that specific event or sample. The relationship between weight and hits is that the weight column is used to adjust the hits column to reflect the true number of events or samples represented in the dataset. # CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Overview

This is the `fbcode/` directory within the fbsource monorepo. Fbcode is where Meta's backend services and AI infrastructure code lives. It's a federation of projects/services that run on their own servers and interoperate through Thrift.

For general fbsource repository guidance (Buck, testing, linting, Sapling, Phabricator/Jellyfish), see the parent `../.claude/CLAUDE.md`.

## Languages and Build System

Fbcode primarily uses C++ and Python. From within the fbcode directory, relative Buck paths also work:

```bash
buck build //path/to:target
```

## Testing

Tests are typically located at `$(dirname F)/test(s)/FTest.ext` for file `F.ext`.

### Expect Tests

When updating expected values in expect tests, run the test target without specifying a specific test name - passing the test name won't work for updating expectations.

## Configerator Config Access

NEVER read configs directly from `/var/facebook/configerator-client/`,
`/var/facebook/configerator-mount/`, or any path under them. These directories are
FUSE mounts of the local configerator proxy — an
internal implementation detail, not a stable API. Direct reads bypass subscription
tracking, canary support, update callbacks, signature verification, and override logic.

Use the official Configerator Consumption API instead:
- C++: `ConfigeratorConfig<T>` (`//common/config:configerator_config`)
- Python: `ConfigeratorConfig` or `ConfigeratorClient` (`//configerator/distribution/api/py:client`)
- Testing: `ScopedConfigeratorFake` (C++) or `ConfigeratorFake` (Python)

The only legitimate direct-filesystem readers are bootstrapping infrastructure with
circular dependencies (e.g., StaticVerifierKeyProvider, MetalOS early boot).

## Code Style

- Be consistent with local conventions - fbcode is huge with varying styles
- Files with the `@​generated` tag should never be edited manually
- Open source directories have files with licenses at the top; the `facebook/` subdirectory within them is not open sourced
- Default copyright: `// (c) Meta Platforms, Inc. and affiliates. Confidential and proprietary.`

# An Introduction to the Logger Framework for AI Agents (Fbcode/Thrift)

Logger is a privacy-aware and type-safe framework for logging analytics, observability or training data from any server application to a data warehouse (such as Hive, Scuba, ODS, Scribe, etc.). In fbcode, Logger uses Thrift IDL for defining logger configurations and supports C++, Python, Rust, and Go languages. Logger employs Scribe as its transport layer and is built with privacy awareness, supporting privacy policies and annotations. Key features include sampling, batching, data validation, multiple logging destinations, a robust testing framework with Staging Logger, automated resource curation through Automatic Actualization, and an easy-to-access UI called Logger Console, available via the bunnylol command.

The thrift logger authoring workflow is purely in fbcode. You don't need to work on WWW. Note: the loggers in fbcode will only work in fbcode and can't work with data from mobile logging via Falco. Ignore the rules file in `www/.llms/rules/logger.md`

## Introduction

# Why Loggers

Loggers are the preferred way to log data to Hive, Scuba, ODS, Scribe, etc. The Logger framework provides a number of benefits over ad-hoc logging:

1. **Privacy-aware**: Logger supports privacy policies and annotations, ensuring that data when logged is compliant with privacy regulations.
2. **Type-safe**: Logger provides type safety for data being logged, ensuring that data is logged in the statically of the correct type and avoiding errors at runtime.
3. **Flexible**: Logger supports multiple logging destinations, including Hive, Scuba, ODS and Scribe.
4. **Flexible-data-management**: Logger supports CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) on the data being logged, allowing for flexible data management.
5. **Framework-provided-core-strategies**: Logger supports sampling, batching, derivation and data validation out of the box. These strategies are supported by the framework as a first class citizen.
6. **Testing**: Logger provides a robust testing framework with Staging Logger, which allows you to test your logger config before it is pushed to production.
7. **Automated resource curation**: Logger supports automated resource curation through Automatic Actualization, which automatically updates the underlying resources (e.g. Hive tables, Scuba datasets, etc.) based on the changes in the logger config.
8. **Easy-to-access UI**: Logger Console is an easy-to-access UI that provides a centralized view of the logger, including its configuration, usage, and performance metrics.
9. **Code generation**: LoggerCli codegen generates code for the logger config in almost all major languages: Hack, Python, C++, and more. Note: Java is not supported for thrift-authored loggers.

# LoggerConfig

A LoggerConfig is a configuration file that defines the schema of the data being logged. When you create a LoggerConfig, you define the fields that you want to log, their types, and any additional metadata such as sampling rates, privacy policies, and annotations. The LoggerConfig is the source of truth for the data being logged, and it is used to generate the code that actually logs the data in C++, Python, Rust, or Go.

In fbcode, you define a `foo_logger_config.thrift` file using Thrift IDL. The authoring workflow is purely in fbcode - you don't need to work on WWW. Note: the loggers will only work in fbcode and can't be used in WWW.

## Types of Logger configurations

Broadly, there are 2 main types of loggers configurations in fbcode:
1. **LoggerConfig**: The most common type of logger configuration. It is a Thrift struct that defines the schema and destinations. It is used to log data to single or multiple destinations (hive and/or scuba and/or ods and/or raw scribe).
2. **MulticatLogger**: A multicat logger logs data to multiple destinations (like multiple hive tables) instead of a single destination. On logging, one of the destinations is selected explicitly at the callsite. You define a multicat logger by specifying `multicat_hive`, `multicat_scuba`, or `multicat_raw_scribe` in the LoggerInfo annotation.

Note: Multiplexed logger config groups are NOT supported in thrift logger authoring flow. Use multicat instead or define multiplexed loggers via the Hack authoring flow.

## Generated Loggers

The Logger framework generates code for the LoggerConfig in C++, Python, Rust, and Go. In C++, these look like `FooLoggerConfig/Logger.h` and `FooLoggerConfig/Logger.cpp`. In Python, these are classes that provide logging methods. Java logger is not supported for thrift-authored loggers.

The generated code provides a simple API for logging data, and it handles all the underlying details such as sampling, batching, data validation, and privacy checks. To make changes to the LoggerConfig and update the generated code, you can use the `loggercli codegen <YourLoggerConfig>` command. The codegen command will validate your logger config and generate logger libraries in the languages you specified in the LoggerInfo annotation.

# Code Examples

It is usually a good idea to look for example code in the directory you are in because that should contain interactions with relevant Loggers.

## Defining Logger Configs
See `fbsource/fbcode/dsi/logger/thrift_configs/FbcodeExampleLoggerConfig/fbcode_example_logger_config.thrift` for an example of how to define a logger config, and `fbsource/fbcode/dsi/logger/thrift_configs/SimpleFbcodeExampleLoggerConfig/simple_fbcode_example_logger_config.thrift` for a simpler example. Also see the corresponding BUCK files for build configuration examples. See `fbcode/dsi/logger/thrift_configs/ExampleThriftCustomMulticatLoggerConfig/example_thrift_custom_multicat_logger_config.thrift` for an example of a multicat logger config.

## Creating a new Logger Config
Use the script to generate a logger config template:
```bash
loggercli new <YourLoggerConfig> <your_oncall_shortname>
```
This will generate `your_logger_config.thrift` and the corresponding BUCK file for you to get started.

## Basic Thrift Logger Config Structure
```thrift
@ownership.Ownership{oncall_shortname="your_oncall"}
@logger_config.Description{description="Your logger description"}
@destination.HiveInfo{
  table_name="your_table",
  table_namespace="your_namespace",
  primary_retention = 30,
  retention_reason = "your retention reason",
}
@destination.ScubaInfo{
  dataset_name="your_dataset",
  dataset_namespace="your_namespace",
}
@logger_config.LoggerInfo {
  hive = logger_config.LoggerDestinationInfo{sample_rate=1.0},
  scuba = logger_config.LoggerDestinationInfo{sample_rate=0.5},
  lang = [logger_config.Language.CPP, logger_config.Language.PY3],
}
struct YourLoggerConfig {
  @logger_config.Description {description = "Example string field"}
  @field_info.LoggerFieldInfo {}
  1: string example_string;

  @logger_config.Description {description = "Example int field"}
  @field_info.LoggerFieldInfo {nullable = false}
  2: i64 example_int;
}
```

## Rules when regenerating code
The loggercli command line tool is the primary way to interact with LoggerConfig and their generated logger code. Never modify the generated logger code directly, instead have the changes be done to the configuration (LoggerConfig) and use the CLI to regenerate the code.

`loggercli codegen` => Run code generation for the given config. You always need to run this after any change to your config.

Steps to create a new logger:
1. You must run `loggercli new <YourLoggerConfig> <your_oncall_shortname>` to create a new logger. You must specify a valid oncall and namespace. This will generate a new `your_logger_config.thrift` file.
2. Update the LoggerConfig in your `.thrift` file.
3. Run `loggercli codegen <YourLoggerConfig>` to validate the logger config and generate the logger lib.

Steps to regenerate code for adding / updating the LoggerConfig:
1. Add/update the LoggerConfig in your `.thrift` file
2. Run `loggercli codegen <YourLoggerConfig>` to re-generate the generated code of the loggers. The changes should appear in the generated C++/Python/Rust/Go code in your build directories.
3. Done. You can use your build system (buck2) to check for any compilation errors.

Steps to delete a logger:
1. Run `loggercli delete-generated-code <YourLoggerConfig>` to remove the loggers generated files.
2. Remove the `<YourLoggerConfig>.thrift` file if it exists.
3. Done. You can use buck2 to check for any compilation errors.

A few things to note:
- Ownership and descriptions are required for all logger configs.
- Destination info defines the destinations to log to. At least one destination is required.

## Testing your logger config
Use [Staging Logger](https://www.internalfb.com/intern/wiki/Server_Logging/Logger_Config_Lifecycle/Testing_LoggerConfig/Testing_Changes_:_Staging/) to test your LoggerConfig changes in temp tables without touching prod:
```bash
loggercli stage actualize-stage <YourLoggerConfig>
```

## Using Loggers

### C++ Example
```cpp
#include "dsi/logger/cpp/generated/YourLoggerConfig/Logger.h"

// Create logger instance
logger::YourLoggerConfigLogger logger;

// Set fields and log
logger.setExampleString("Hello World");
logger.setExampleInt(123);
logger.log();
```

### Python Example
```python
from dsi.logger.py3.generated.YourLoggerConfig.Logger import YourLoggerConfigLogger

# Create logger instance
logger = YourLoggerConfigLogger()

# Set fields and log
logger.set_example_string("Hello World")
logger.set_example_int(123)
logger.log()
```

### Multicat Logger Usage
For multicat loggers, specify the destination:
```cpp
MyMulticatLogger log;
log.selectHiveDestination("your_hive_table_name");
log.selectRawScribeDestination("your_scribe_category");
log.selectScubaDestination("your_scuba_dataset");
```

## Best Practices
* Logger config files should be placed in:
  - `fbsource/fbcode/dsi/logger/thrift_configs/<YourLoggerConfig>/`
  - Your project's specific logger directory

* Always use `loggercli codegen` after making changes to your logger config
* Use staging logger to test changes before production
* Follow the privacy annotation requirements for your data
* Use canonical fields when possible to auto-generate common values
* Remember to update BUCK files when adding dependencies, if you cannot find a BUCK file, look for TARGETS files

## Field Types Reference
- `string` → Hive: string, Scuba: normal
- `i64/i32/i16` → Hive: bigint/int/smallint, Scuba: int
- `bool` → Hive: bool, Scuba: int
- `double/float` → Hive: double/float, Scuba: float32
- `map` → Hive: map, Scuba: tagset
- `list/set` → Hive: array, Scuba: normvector
- `struct` → Hive: struct, Scuba: NOT supported
- `enum` → Hive: string, Scuba: normal

## Monitoring and debugging
For debugging, check the [where is my data wiki](https://www.internalfb.com/intern/wiki/Server_Logging/Loggers_and_Logger_Features/Logger_Tools/Where_is_my_data/) or post in the Logger Users group.
